title: ShapleyPass: Game-Theoretic Attribution and Interaction Analysis of Compiler Optimization Passes
description: We propose the first application of Shapley values and Shapley interaction indices from cooperative game theory to compiler optimization passes. By modeling pass selection as a cooperative game where each optimization pass is a player and the coalition value is the resulting performance improvement, we compute principled attributions of each pass's contribution and quantify pairwise synergies and antagonisms between passes. This interaction landscape enables an interpretable, interaction-guided greedy pass selector that matches standard optimization levels with significantly fewer passes.
motivation: The compiler phase-ordering problem has been studied for four decades, yet current approaches either search for good pass sequences using ML/RL (treating the pipeline as a black box) or analyze structural dependencies between passes (missing performance-level interactions). No existing work provides a principled, axiomatic framework for attributing performance gains to individual passes and their combinations. Understanding WHY certain pass combinations work—not just finding good sequences—is essential for compiler design, debugging optimization regressions, and guiding more efficient search strategies.
proposed_approach: We define a cooperative game where LLVM optimization passes are players and the characteristic function maps pass subsets to their optimization outcome (instruction count reduction or speedup). We compute approximate Shapley values via Monte Carlo permutation sampling (1000 permutations, ~50 passes) and Shapley interaction indices using KernelSHAP-IQ to quantify pairwise synergies/antagonisms. The resulting 'pass interaction atlas' reveals which passes are universally valuable, which are program-specific, and which pairs exhibit strong synergy or antagonism. We then use this interaction landscape to build a greedy forward-selection algorithm (ShapleyPass-Select) that scores candidate passes by combining their individual Shapley value with their interaction synergy with the current selected set.

related_work.title: Beyond the Phase Ordering Problem: Finding the Globally Optimal Code w.r.t. Optimization Phases
related_work.authors: Yu Wang, Hongyu Chen, Ke Wang
related_work.relation: Proves phase ordering alone is insufficient for optimal code. Motivates our deeper analysis of pass interactions.

related_work.title: Efficient compiler optimization by modeling passes dependence
related_work.venue: CCF Transactions on High Performance Computing
related_work.relation: Models pass dependencies via source code and performance analysis with ad-hoc clustering. We provide axiomatic game-theoretic attribution instead.

related_work.title: CompilerDream: Learning a Compiler World Model for General Code Optimization
related_work.authors: Chaoyi Deng, Jialong Wu, Ningya Feng, Jianmin Wang, Mingsheng Long
related_work.relation: State-of-the-art RL approach to pass selection. Treats passes as a black box; our analysis could inform their reward shaping.

related_work.title: MiCOMP: Mitigating the Compiler Phase-Ordering Problem Using Optimization Sub-Sequences and Machine Learning
related_work.authors: Michael R. Jantz, Prasad A. Kulkarni
related_work.relation: Clusters passes into sub-sequences using co-occurrence. Our Shapley interactions provide a principled alternative to ad-hoc clustering.

related_work.title: MLGO: A Machine Learning Guided Compiler Optimizations Framework
related_work.relation: Integrates ML into LLVM for individual pass decisions (inlining). Complementary; our framework could guide which passes to target with ML.

related_work.title: CompilerGym: Robust, Performant Compiler Optimization Environments for AI Research
related_work.relation: Provides RL environments for compiler optimization. We use their benchmark programs and build upon their infrastructure.

related_work.title: shapiq: Shapley Interactions for Machine Learning
related_work.relation: Provides the computational framework for Shapley interaction indices that we adapt to compiler pass analysis.

related_work.title: KernelSHAP-IQ: Weighted Least-Square Optimization for Shapley Interactions
related_work.relation: Efficient method for computing higher-order Shapley interactions. Key algorithmic tool for our interaction computation.

related_work.title: Mitigating the compiler optimization phase-ordering problem using machine learning
related_work.relation: Foundational work applying ML to phase ordering. We provide explanatory analysis complementing their predictive approach.

hypothesis: Compiler optimization passes exhibit significant non-additive interactions quantifiable by Shapley interaction indices. A Shapley-guided greedy pass selector leveraging these interactions can match LLVM -O3 code size reduction to within 2% while using at most 50% of the passes, outperforming both naive top-k selection and random search baselines.
success_criteria: At least 30% of pass pairs show statistically significant non-zero Shapley interaction indices (p < 0.05 after correction)
Interaction-based pass clustering differs meaningfully from source-code dependency clustering (adjusted Rand index < 0.5)
ShapleyPass-Select matches -O3 code size within 2% using at most 50% of passes
Cross-program interaction consistency: average Spearman correlation > 0.3 within program clusters
ShapleyPass-Select outperforms random search and top-k Shapley baselines given equal compilation budget

feasibility.compute: CPU-only: each LLVM opt invocation takes 10-100ms on small benchmarks. 50,000 compilations per program x 53 programs = ~2.65M compilations total. With 2 cores and optimizations: 6-8 hours.
feasibility.data: cBench (23 programs), PolyBench/C (30 kernels) - freely available standard benchmarks
feasibility.tools: LLVM 17+ (opt tool), Python, shapiq library for Shapley computation
